data.title: Appian outlines AI-driven growth with new pricing tiers and 14%-16% Q2 cloud revenue growth target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Appian Corporation (APPN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matt Calkins highlighted that Appian's cloud subscription revenue increased 15% year-over-year to $99.8 million, with total revenue reaching $166.4 million, an 11% increase year-over-year.
  • The company has made significant advancements in AI adoption, with 70% of cloud customers integrating AI and a 7.9x increase in production AI usage compared to the same period in 2024. Additionally, revenue from AI-inclusive pricing tiers doubled quarter-over-quarter to $9 million.
  • A new Chief Financial Officer, Serge Tanjga, will join the team later in May, bringing extensive financial experience from MongoDB. Mark Lynch, the interim CFO, will remain on the Board of Directors.
  • Appian’s annual customer conference, Appian World, emphasized the company’s focus on AI integration. Customers from industries like insurance and healthcare shared their success stories, with examples of improved accuracy, efficiency, and cost reductions through Appian AI.
  • Calkins introduced two internal metrics: the weighted rule of 40, which combines growth and margin, and a go-to-market productivity measure. The weighted rule of 40 score for Q1 was 27.
  • CFO Mark Lynch noted that the company exceeded guidance on cloud revenue, total revenue, and adjusted EBITDA, with adjusted EBITDA reaching $16.8 million.

Outlook

  • Appian provided Q2 2025 guidance, projecting cloud subscription revenue between $101 million and $103 million, representing 14% to 16% growth year-over-year. Total revenue is expected to range between $158 million and $162 million, with an adjusted EBITDA loss between (-$5 million) and (-$2 million).
  • For the full year 2025, the company raised the high end of its guidance for cloud subscription revenue to $423 million and total revenue to $688 million, reflecting growth rates of 14%-15% and 10%-12%, respectively. Adjusted EBITDA for the year is expected to be between $40 million and $46 million.
  • Management emphasized the cautious optimism surrounding U.S. federal government spending due to market uncertainty but reported strong federal bookings growth of 59% year-over-year in Q1.

Financial Results

  • Subscriptions revenue represented 81% of total revenue, with a non-GAAP gross margin of 78% and a subscriptions non-GAAP gross profit margin of 89%.
  • Non-GAAP net income was $9.8 million, or $0.13 per diluted share, compared to a loss of (-$4.9 million) in Q1 2024. Cash and cash equivalents increased to $199.7 million as of March 31, 2025.
  • Deferred revenue grew 16% year-over-year, reaching $262.5 million. Cloud net new annual contract value (ACV) bookings accounted for 82% of total net new software bookings during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Sanjit Singh, Morgan Stanley: Asked about federal government spending and its impact on Q3. Calkins clarified that no significant pull-forward activity occurred in Q1 and expressed cautious optimism regarding federal spending trends.
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: Inquired about differentiation in AI agent deployment amidst market noise. Calkins emphasized that Appian focuses on practical, results-driven AI applications, contrasting with competitors' hype-driven approaches.
  • Steve Enders, Citi: Asked about monetization of AI tiers. Calkins noted early customer willingness to pay for AI functionality, with AI doubling the total addressable market for process automation.
  • Nick Altmann, Scotiabank: Queried about AI revenue contribution in 2025. Calkins highlighted ongoing campaigns to transition customers to higher AI-inclusive pricing tiers, with a focus on adopting consumption-based pricing models.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about federal government spending uncertainty and AI adoption trends but acknowledged strong financial performance and AI growth metrics.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in its prepared remarks, supported by robust Q1 results and new AI-related metrics. Calkins emphasized the practicality of Appian's AI strategy, with repeated mentions of "practical" and "results-driven" approaches.
  • Sentiment compared to the previous quarter was consistent, with cautious optimism expressed regarding federal spending and solid confidence in AI adoption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Cloud subscription revenue growth slowed to 15% in Q1 2025 from 19% in Q4 2024, while total revenue remained stable.
  • Federal government bookings growth accelerated to 59% year-over-year in Q1, compared to strong yet unspecified growth in Q4.
  • The retention rate for cloud subscription revenue decreased to 112% in Q1 from 116% the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted macroeconomic uncertainty and changes within the federal government as potential risks but noted no significant disruptions to the sales pipeline.
  • Analysts questioned the durability of recent sales productivity gains and the potential impact of AI-related pricing transitions on revenue.
  • The retention rate decline and potential variability in deferred revenue growth were noted as areas for investor attention.

Final Takeaway

Appian reported strong Q1 2025 results, driven by 15% growth in cloud subscription revenue and significant progress in AI adoption, with monetization from AI-inclusive pricing tiers doubling quarter-over-quarter. The company raised its full-year guidance for cloud subscription revenue and adjusted EBITDA, reflecting confidence in its strategy. While federal government spending and macroeconomic uncertainty remain risks, Appian's focus on results-driven AI applications and strategic pricing models positions it for continued growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Americold signals cautious outlook with AFFO guidance range of $1.42-$1.52 for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Americold Realty Trust, Inc. (COLD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO George Chappelle highlighted the company’s response to tariff impacts, stating that "the direct impacts are relatively modest" but acknowledged significant indirect effects on consumer confidence, causing adjustments in customer product portfolios and reduced inventory levels. This led to a revision in the company’s annual outlook.
  • Same-store economic occupancy declined by approximately 270 basis points sequentially from Q4 2024, attributed to seasonality and market softness. Rent and storage revenue from fixed commitment contracts increased to 60%, achieving a goal set three years ago.
  • Labor metrics improved significantly, with the permanent-to-temp hours ratio rising to 78:22 and associate turnover reduced to 29%, reflecting enhanced hiring and training practices.
  • The company announced a dividend increase of approximately 5%, bringing the quarterly dividend to $0.23 per share.
  • CFO Jay Wells explained that the 2025 AFFO per share guidance has been revised to a range of $1.42 to $1.52 due to macroeconomic uncertainties.

Outlook

  • Management now expects full-year 2025 AFFO per share to range between $1.42 and $1.52, lower than prior guidance due to slower inventory builds and cautious customer behavior.
  • Same-store constant currency revenue growth is forecasted to range from flat to 2%, with economic occupancy expected to decline by 200 basis points compared to 2024.
  • Rent and storage revenue per economic occupied pallet and services revenue per throughput pallet are both projected to grow between 1% and 2%.
  • The company anticipates the seasonal inventory build to occur in the back half of the year, though muted compared to historical trends.

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 AFFO per share was $0.34. This performance aligned with expectations but reflected headwinds from lower inventory levels and incremental costs of $7 million related to technology licensing and labor expenses tied to Project Orion.
  • Q1 same-store warehouse services margins improved by 110 basis points year-over-year to 11.2%, tracking above the annual target of 12%.
  • Rent and storage revenue per economic occupied pallet increased by approximately 2% year-over-year, while services revenue per throughput pallet rose by over 3%.
  • The Houston acquisition in March added 36,000 pallet positions, with a total investment of $127 million, including equipment upgrades. Stabilization of this asset is expected in Q1 2027.

Q&A

  • Samir Khanal, Bank of America: Asked about the impact of tariffs and slowdown in demand. CEO Chappelle noted that the "environment is completely different from when we first announced our guidance," with cautious customer behavior reflecting lower consumer confidence.
  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Questioned the widening gap between physical and economic occupancy. CEO Chappelle emphasized aggressive growth in fixed commitments, now at 60% of rent and storage revenue, and stated that the gap is consistent with customer operational needs.
  • Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank: Inquired about the development pipeline. Rob Chambers highlighted the $1 billion pipeline, including the Kansas City and Port St. John projects, both strategically positioned to optimize supply chains.
  • Ki Bin Kim, Truist Securities: Asked about non-same-store NOI guidance. CFO Wells explained that ramp-up costs for new developments offset early-stage contributions, with stabilization benefits expected in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern over reduced economic occupancy and cautious customer behavior, reflecting a slightly negative sentiment overall. Questions focused on inventory levels, pricing pressure, and the economic outlook.
  • Management maintained a balanced tone, emphasizing operational improvements and resilience despite headwinds. Statements such as "we are confident" and "our view of the market remains strong" reflected cautious optimism.
  • Compared to Q4 2024, management’s tone shifted to acknowledge heightened external risks, but confidence in strategic initiatives and labor improvements persisted.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • AFFO guidance was revised downward from the Q4 2024 range of $1.51-$1.59, reflecting macroeconomic challenges.
  • Fixed commitment revenue increased to 60%, compared to 59% in Q4 2024, demonstrating continued success in securing long-term customer contracts.
  • Economic occupancy weakened by 270 basis points sequentially, reflecting normalized seasonality and reduced demand.
  • Management’s tone was slightly more cautious, emphasizing external economic risks and tempered expectations for inventory recovery in the second half of the year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged reduced consumer confidence resulting from tariff impacts and inflation fears as key risks affecting inventory and occupancy levels.
  • Pricing pressure from competitors using aggressive discounting strategies was noted as a challenge.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the widening gap between physical and economic occupancy, though management downplayed operational significance.

Final Takeaway

Americold Realty Trust's Q1 2025 earnings call highlighted operational improvements and strategic advancements despite macroeconomic challenges. The company revised its AFFO guidance downwards to $1.42-$1.52 per share, citing cautious customer behavior and slower inventory builds. Key initiatives in fixed commitments, labor management, and strategic developments such as the Houston acquisition and Port St. John project demonstrate a focus on long-term value creation. However, external risks, including tariff impacts and pricing competition, remain critical factors to monitor.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Manulife financial outlines growth in Asia sales and $7B remittance amid macro challenges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Manulife Financial Corporation (MFC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Roy Gori highlighted the strong Q1 2025 results and emphasized the momentum from 2024. Asia annual premium equivalent (APE) sales increased by 50%, driven by robust customer demand and effective execution in the region. Global Wealth and Asset Management (WAM) achieved positive net flows despite market volatility. Core EPS grew by 3%, with normalized growth at 9% after accounting for provisions related to California wildfires and expected credit loss (ECL) adjustments.
  • CFO Colin Simpson noted that APE sales rose by 37% year-over-year, contributing to 36% growth in new business value and 31% growth in new business contractual service margin (CSM). Asia generated record core earnings, with a 7% year-over-year increase. Global WAM maintained robust performance, achieving 24% growth in core earnings.
  • Incoming CEO Phil Witherington underlined the company's strong foundation and diversification, particularly in Asia and Global WAM. He expressed confidence in sustaining the current growth trajectory.
  • Outgoing CEO Gori remarked on the transformation achieved during his tenure, including a reduced risk profile and enhanced shareholder returns. He assured stakeholders of a seamless transition of leadership.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated confidence in Asia's growth, driven by strong demand for savings and protection solutions. Despite macroeconomic uncertainties, they expect demand to persist across key markets like Hong Kong, Singapore, China, and Japan.
  • CFO Simpson affirmed that the company is well-capitalized, with a LICAT ratio of 137% and a financial leverage ratio of 23.9%, below the 25% medium-term target. This positions Manulife to navigate volatile markets effectively while pursuing growth opportunities.
  • The company’s capital return strategy includes share buybacks and dividends, supported by the robust capital generation from reinsurance transactions and core business operations.

Financial Results

  • Core EPS increased by 3%, supported by growth in Asia and Global WAM. The normalized growth rate was 9%, excluding charges from California wildfire-related provisions and ECL adjustments.
  • Asia's APE sales surged by 50% year-over-year, with strong contributions from Hong Kong, Singapore, Japan, and mainland China.
  • Global WAM achieved $500M in net inflows and 24% growth in core earnings, demonstrating resilience despite market challenges. The core EBITDA margin expanded to 28.4%, up by 290 basis points year-over-year.
  • The company reported a $781M non-core charge related to the LTC reinsurance transaction and a $208M charge due to lower equity returns.

Q&A

  • John Aiken (Jefferies) inquired about the outlook for Asia sales. Incoming CEO Witherington noted strong demand for savings solutions and generational wealth transfer products, particularly in Hong Kong and Singapore. He expressed cautious optimism for sustained growth in 2025 despite potential macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Doug Young (Desjardins Capital Markets) asked about Japan and Hong Kong sales drivers. Witherington explained that U.S. dollar-denominated products in Japan remain a key offering due to yield differentials, while Hong Kong's sales growth stems from domestic and mainland Chinese visitor demand.
  • Gabriel Dechaine (National Bank Financial) raised concerns regarding mix challenges in Asia. Witherington acknowledged strong demand for savings products but highlighted the potential for future growth in health and protection solutions.
  • Tom MacKinnon (BMO Capital Markets) asked about the company’s appetite for further legacy transactions. Marc Costantini emphasized that recent transactions have successfully de-risked the portfolio, reducing legacy earnings to less than 10% of the total. Additional transactions will be evaluated based on shareholder value.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts remained focused on growth sustainability and macroeconomic risks. The tone was cautiously optimistic, with concerns about potential headwinds in Asia and credit market volatility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing diversification and the strength of the balance sheet. During Q&A, they adopted a measured stance, acknowledging uncertainties while reiterating resilience and growth opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was consistent, with a focus on solidifying the growth trajectory and addressing external challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Asia's sales momentum accelerated further in Q1 2025, driven by a 50% increase in APE sales compared to robust growth highlighted in Q4 2024.
  • Global WAM maintained positive net flows but faced continued pressure in certain segments like retirement.
  • Management tone remained confident, though macroeconomic uncertainties, including trade tensions and interest rate volatility, were more prominently discussed this quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted slightly towards the sustainability of Asia’s growth and potential risks from geopolitical factors.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified heightened macroeconomic volatility as a key challenge, with potential impacts on consumer sentiment and investment markets.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of Asia’s growth and possible delays in higher-margin product sales.
  • The ECL provision increased due to deteriorating economic conditions, though credit experience remained stable.
  • The non-core charges related to reinsurance transactions and equity market returns highlighted potential vulnerabilities in earnings.

Final Takeaway

Manulife Financial delivered a solid performance in Q1 2025, with significant growth in Asia and Global WAM driving results. The company demonstrated resilience amid macroeconomic challenges, supported by a strong capital position and diversified business portfolio. Leadership transitions are expected to proceed smoothly, with a continued focus on executing growth strategies in high-potential regions like Asia. Despite uncertainties, management expressed confidence in sustaining momentum and achieving medium-term targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Warner bros. discovery outlines path to $1.3B streaming EBITDA in 2025 with global expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Zaslav emphasized the company's focus on quality storytelling as a driving factor for success, highlighting strong contributions from HBO, its local language content, and local sports programming. The company added over 5 million subscribers in Q1 2025, bringing its total to 150 million globally, and achieved $339 million in streaming EBITDA. Zaslav stated that Warner Bros. Discovery aims to reach $1.3 billion in streaming EBITDA for 2025, representing an 85% increase over 2024.

  • Zaslav noted the strong performance of the studio segment, citing hit films like "Minecraft Movie" and "Sinners" and upcoming releases including "Final Destination" and the launch of "Superman" in July as part of its 10-year DC branding strategy.

  • CFO Gunnar Wiedenfels highlighted the company's reorganization efforts, which provide visibility into the two-division structure of streaming and studios. He indicated that the company is positioned for flexibility and agility in responding to market opportunities.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its EBITDA target of $1.3 billion for 2025 in the streaming segment and expects to exceed 150 million subscribers by the end of 2025.

  • The company plans to leverage its strong content lineup, including premium franchises from HBO and DC Studios, as well as international expansion, especially in key markets like Germany, the UK, and Italy.

  • Management outlined plans to implement password-sharing measures globally by late 2025 into 2026, which is expected to provide incremental revenue growth.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $339 million in streaming EBITDA for Q1 2025. Over the past 12 months, Warner Bros. Discovery has added more than 22 million subscribers.

  • The studio segment is on track to reach $3 billion in EBITDA, with significant contributions expected from franchises like "Harry Potter," "Superman," and "Game of Thrones." Upcoming releases and an improved slate are expected to bolster performance.

  • Cost controls and a focus on high-quality IP utilization have been emphasized to drive profitability.

Q&A

  • Steven Cahall, Wells Fargo: Questioned the strategic rationale behind the reorganization and its impact on capital structure. Wiedenfels responded that the reorganization enhances transparency and optionality, enabling the company to act quickly on opportunities.

  • Peter Supino, Wolfe Research: Asked about the sports strategy on Max. CEO Zaslav emphasized that the company views sports as a valuable but secondary focus, prioritizing owned IP like DC and Harry Potter for long-term value creation.

  • Bryan Kraft, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about HBO's ability to consistently produce standout hits. Zaslav attributed it to a combination of creative talent and a quality-first approach, reinforced by a strong leadership team.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the company's strategic focus on streaming EBITDA growth and international expansion, with a neutral to slightly positive tone.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout the call, particularly regarding its streaming and studio performance. Zaslav reiterated the company's belief in the strength of its global content strategy and IP portfolio.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained consistent, with optimism about achieving 2025 targets and addressing challenges in the linear network segment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Subscriber additions in Q1 2025 continued at a robust pace, with over 5 million new subscribers, compared to 6.5 million in Q4 2024. Management maintained its focus on achieving 150 million subscribers by the end of 2025.

  • Streaming EBITDA improved significantly, with further guidance indicating sustained growth in this segment.

  • Management's tone and strategic focus on quality storytelling and global streaming expansion were consistent with the prior quarter, although more emphasis was placed on international opportunities and tightening password-sharing measures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges in the linear television segment remain, with ongoing sub declines and rate increases moderating to low single-digits.

  • The company faces competitive pressures in the streaming landscape, particularly in maintaining ARPU amid global expansion and the rollout of ad-supported tiers.

  • Management acknowledged the high costs associated with premium sports rights and signaled a cautious approach to further investments in this area.

Final Takeaway

Warner Bros. Discovery highlighted its focus on quality content and strategic global expansion as key drivers for its streaming and studio businesses. With an ambitious $1.3 billion streaming EBITDA target for 2025 and a strong content slate, the company appears well-positioned to leverage its IP portfolio for sustained growth. Management's emphasis on cost controls, international markets, and password-sharing initiatives underscores its commitment to profitability and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: US Foods targets 4-7% independent restaurant case growth for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: US Foods Holding Corp. (USFD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dave Flitman highlighted that Q1 adjusted EBITDA grew over 9%, with adjusted diluted EPS increasing 26% year-over-year, despite severe weather challenges affecting operations. He pointed out this performance as evidence of the company's strong strategy execution and customer value proposition.
  • Flitman announced that the company’s Board has approved a $1 billion share repurchase program, complementing the $1.3 billion in share buybacks since late 2022.
  • CHEF'STORE operations will be retained for the foreseeable future, as efforts to sell the business faced valuation challenges. Management is focusing on improving CHEF'STORE’s profitability despite its limited contribution (less than 5% of total EBITDA).
  • CFO Dirk Locascio stated that total net sales for Q1 increased to $9.4 billion, supported by a 1.1% rise in case volume and 3.4% from food cost inflation and mix.
  • Locascio noted that adjusted EBITDA per case reached $1.90, reflecting operational leverage and productivity gains.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its fiscal year 2025 guidance, targeting adjusted EBITDA growth of 8%-12% and adjusted diluted EPS growth of 17%-23%.
  • Independent restaurant case growth for 2025 is projected between 2%-5%, with total case growth expected at 1%-3%.
  • Flitman emphasized the company’s confidence in maintaining momentum driven by share gains and productivity improvements, stating, "We remain confident to deliver our new long-range algorithm."
  • The company anticipates further growth in healthcare and hospitality segments, supported by new customer wins totaling over $100 million in annualized revenue.

Financial Results

  • Q1 net sales reached $9.4 billion, reflecting a 4.5% increase year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 18 basis points to 4.2%, driven by improved gross profit and operating expense productivity.
  • Independent restaurant case growth was 2.5%, with healthcare and hospitality case growth at 6.1% and 3.6%, respectively.
  • Locascio reported a 9.3% year-over-year rise in adjusted EBITDA to $389 million, driven by gains in gross profit and cost savings initiatives.

Q&A

  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo: Asked about self-help measures and their impact on maintaining profitability amid choppy macroeconomic conditions. CEO Flitman responded that the company’s initiatives in operating expenses and gross margin management are key drivers, reinforcing confidence in achieving guidance.
  • Kelly Bania, BMO Capital Markets: Inquired about expense-saving measures. CFO Locascio explained that $30 million in additional cost actions have been initiated for 2025, complementing $120 million in savings from the prior year.
  • Lauren Silberman, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the cadence of independent case growth. Locascio noted strong net new account generation in April, supporting an expected acceleration in growth during the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic, focusing on independent case growth and margin pressures. Questions reflected concerns about macroeconomic headwinds and the sustainability of recent improvements.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing execution, cost controls, and share gains. Phrases like "We are confident" and "We have multiple levers to pull" highlighted their assurance in achieving targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Compared to Q4 2024, Q1 2025 saw more pronounced weather-related disruptions, which impacted independent restaurant case growth. However, healthcare and hospitality segments continued to exhibit robust performance.
  • Management tone remained consistent, with a focus on executing self-help initiatives and maintaining profitability.
  • Share repurchase plans were expanded significantly, signaling increased shareholder returns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Severe weather events were highlighted as a persistent operational challenge, particularly for independent restaurant case growth.
  • Tariff-related uncertainties could impact imported product costs, though management noted that only a small portion of purchases are affected.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential competitive pressures and macroeconomic volatility affecting restaurant traffic.

Final Takeaway

US Foods delivered strong profitability in Q1 2025 despite challenging conditions. Expanded share repurchase plans, consistent healthcare and hospitality growth, and robust cost management initiatives position the company well for the year ahead. Management remains confident in achieving guidance targets, supported by gains in independent case volumes and operational efficiencies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. consumers boost inflation expectations for three-year horizon: NY Fed
data.description:
Consumers increased their expectations for inflation over the three-year time horizon, but apparently expect inflation to settle down further out, according to the New York Fed's Survey of Consumer Expectations. Meanwhile, they're expecting to feel the squeeze of spending outpacing income growth in the year ahead.

Median inflation expectations for the three-year timeframe rose 0.2 percentage point to 3.2%, the highest reading since July 2022. But the median outlook for inflation five years ahead decreased by 0.2 pp to 2.7%. Consumers' median expectation for inflation a year from now was unchanged at 3.6%, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York said.

In assessing their household finances, consumers reduced their expectations for year-ahead earnings growth by 0.3 pp to 2.5%, the lowest reading since December 2023. Similarly, the median expected growth in household income fell by 0.2 pp to 2.6% in April, the lowest number since April 2021.

At the same time, they expect to spend more. Median household nominal spending growth expectations climbed 0.3 pp to 5.2%. With that, the average perceived probability of missing a minimum debt payment over the next three months also rose 0.3 pp to 13.9%.

Consumers are also seeing labor market conditions softening. The mean perceived probability of finding a job in the next three months, if one's current job was lost, fell by 1.9 pp to 49.2%, the lowest level since March 2021, the NY Fed said.

However, they're a bit more optimistic that they won't be fired. The mean perceived probability of losing one's job in the next 12 months decreased by 0.4 pp to 15.3%.

• GlobalTech press release (OTCPK:GLTK): Q1 Net Revenue increased to $4.3 million, up 17.3%.
  • Net loss per share of $0.01.

data.title: Siemens Energy posts strong jump in quarterly profit, sees limited hit from U.S. tariffs
data.description:
Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) +1.7% in German trading after reporting a nearly fivefold surge in FQ2 net profit and said it expects a limited direct impact from U.S. tariffs in H2 of its fiscal year, citing price increases for new orders as well as a drive to find more local suppliers for parts it needs in the U.S., the company's single biggest market.

Calling the tariffs "annoying but manageable," CEO Christian Bruch said most of Siemens Energy's (OTCPK:SMEGF) orders contained price adjustment clauses that were cushioning the fallout from import tariffs announced by President Trump last month.

While currently forecasting a net profit of up to €1B (~$1.1B) for its fiscal year ending September 30, Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) said it expects "up to a high double-digit million euro" hit to its FY 2025 net profit, quantifying for the first time the impact of the U.S. import tariffs.

The company fleshed out steps to cushion the impact of the tariffs, including measures to buy more supplies locally and better leverage its U.S. "footprint for production, processing and repair of key components."

For Q2, the company reported net profit jumped to €501M, or €0.50/share, from €108M, or €0.08/share, in the year-earlier quarter, well above the €217M analyst consensus estimate in an LSEG poll, while revenues rose 21% to €9.96B, as previously reported.

data.title: Boeing's shares rise as U.S.-U.K. trade deal includes $10B plane order
data.description:
Boeing's (NYSE:BA) shares moved sharply upward after U.S. Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick said at a White House press conference that an upcoming trade deal between the United States and United Kingdom includes a $10 billion order for Boeing planes.

More to come....

data.title: YETI outlines 2025 strategy with 30 new product launches and accelerated supply chain transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: YETI Holdings, Inc. (YETI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matt Reintjes highlighted three priorities for 2025: accelerating product innovation, transforming the supply chain to minimize reliance on China, and maintaining operational discipline to sustain a strong balance sheet. Reintjes emphasized the impact of supply chain diversification efforts, stating that 90% of U.S. drinkware production will be ex-China by year-end.
  • Management reported a record pipeline of over 30 new products planned for release in 2025, surpassing the 24 products launched in 2024. Innovations include insulated sports jugs, beach chairs, and food-related products.
  • CFO Mike McMullen noted sales growth of 3% to $351 million in Q1, driven by strong performance in Coolers & Equipment and international markets. He also mentioned a significant decline in SG&A expenses compared to internal projections due to cost management efforts.
  • Reintjes expressed confidence in long-term growth despite current macro challenges, stating that YETI is focusing on strategic investments such as new testing and innovation centers in Asia and Denver.

Outlook

  • Management expects full-year sales growth of 1%-4% for 2025, incorporating a 300-basis-point impact from supply chain disruptions as drinkware production is transitioned out of China.
  • Gross margins for 2025 are projected at approximately 54%, reflecting the impact of tariffs. Adjusted EPS guidance was revised to $1.96-$2.02, down from prior expectations of $2.90-$2.95, driven by supply disruptions and tariff costs.
  • International growth is projected at 15%-20%, with strong contributions anticipated from Europe and Japan, where commercial sales are set to begin in Q2 2025.

Financial Results

  • Coolers & Equipment sales grew 17% year-over-year, reaching $140.2 million in Q1. This marks the fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth for this category.
  • Drinkware sales decreased by 4% to $205.6 million due to supply chain challenges and a competitive U.S. market. However, international drinkware sales achieved double-digit growth.
  • Direct-to-consumer (DTC) sales rose 4% to $196.2 million, bolstered by strong performance on Amazon and in corporate sales.
  • Inventory levels decreased by 9% year-over-year, reflecting strategic management amid supply chain disruptions. The company ended Q1 with $259 million in cash.

Q&A

  • Randy Konik, Jefferies: Asked about the cadence of new product launches and their reception compared to prior years. Reintjes responded that YETI’s innovation pace will increase, with over 30 new products planned for 2025, supported by supply chain transitions and operational improvements.
  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the outlook for the drinkware business and wholesale demand. Reintjes explained that the company is diversifying the drinkware portfolio to mitigate market concentration risks and noted strong wholesale partnerships.
  • Peter Benedict, Baird: Questioned pricing strategies amid tariff impacts. Reintjes stated that the primary mitigation strategy is accelerating supply chain transitions, with targeted pricing adjustments to manage costs effectively.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about supply chain disruptions and tariff-related cost pressures. Questions focused on inventory management, product launches, and competitive dynamics in the drinkware market.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing long-term growth opportunities and strategic investments. However, a more cautious tone emerged during discussions of tariff impacts and consumer demand uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone appeared more defensive, particularly regarding revised guidance and supply chain challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sales growth slowed to 3% in Q1 2025 from 7% in Q4 2024, attributed to drinkware supply chain issues and a competitive market.
  • Adjusted EPS guidance for 2025 was significantly reduced, reflecting tariff impacts and delayed product launches, compared to the optimistic outlook in Q4 2024.
  • Management’s tone shifted to a focus on navigating near-term challenges, contrasting with the more expansive growth narrative in the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing supply chain disruptions, particularly in drinkware, as a key risk for 2025. Efforts to mitigate these include diversifying production and decreasing reliance on China.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the competitive drinkware market and potential consumer demand softness in the U.S.
  • Tariff impacts remain a significant headwind, with $100 million in gross tariff costs projected for 2025.

Final Takeaway

YETI Holdings is navigating a transitional year in 2025, marked by aggressive supply chain transformations, a record number of new product launches, and strategic investments in global expansion. While the company faces headwinds from tariffs and supply disruptions, its focus on innovation and operational discipline positions it for long-term growth. Investors should monitor the execution of supply chain changes and the market reception to new products, particularly in the competitive drinkware segment.
